paragraphs.body: I have been exposed to a multitude of formulas in my life across all subjects including, math, physics, chemistry, biology, English, and music. Due to this, when I think of formulas, I don’t strictly relate them to math. I wonder why formulas have been so focused on mathematics when they relate to so many other subjects? I’m curious if anyone else formulates formulas to use on a day-to-day basis for normal things like talking to people? What caused me to start doing it? Is it a good habit or bad habit?

paragraphs.body: I don’t like being the only voice. Even after playing the sax for eight years, I still get nervous to perform by myself. One of the solos I remember still was during my senior year of high school when I played the song “Things Ain’t What They Used to Be” by Duke Ellington at a Jazz festival. All my life I’ve been content being second Alto, hiding behind the screaming dynamics of the first Alto’s melody with underlying harmonies. But this year my teacher decided to put me in at first chair. Even before my solo began, I started getting shaky, my right leg began twitching and my heel repeatedly struck the floor like I was trying to kickstart my brain. As I approached the first four counts before my entrance it felt as if all the water was being redirected from my body straight to my palms, my fingers began to slip off the keys and I started worrying I was going to drop my horn on stage completely disregarding in my mind the idea of a neck strap which would make dropping it impossible. On my first note, I could barely hear anything. My heart was beating so loudly in my head I couldn’t even follow the booming accents from the kick drum or the stabs of the piano. However, even with all these distractions, I still managed to come in with the same lick that I did every time, a high F natural. This high F natural, like many things in my life, was part of a formula I ran through my head thousands of times before. I thought about it before I fingered the note, before I heard the band’s first hit, before I stood up and walked to the mic, before the concert even started, and probably before I went to bed the night before. Running these formulas through my head keep me locked in and help bring me back down to earth especially in moments like this where I feel like I’m barely hanging on.

annotations.text: still managed to come in with the same lick that I did every time, a high F natural.

paragraphs.body: I need to make my own formulas sometimes because I never had formal lessons on the saxophone, except for an introduction to the instrument in fifth grade from my music teacher Dr. Selfridge. From then on, I’ve taught myself most of what I know about the saxophone including all the odd qualities of this only brass woodwind. The saxophone, invented in the 1840s by Adolphe Sax, was originally intended for use in orchestras and concert bands. Nowadays it is seen more as a jazz instrument due to its influence and unique sound that fit the genre so well. The sax is a very versatile instrument as well, it can create a soft breathy almost whisper like sound at lower dynamics all the way to a scream at higher dynamics that it shares a lot of characteristics with the human voice. The instrument itself is designed very odd, most people pick it up the first time and can’t find the “home keys” even though they’re marked with concave white pearls. The shape of the instrument and how you hold it doesn’t click at first and apparently, I’ve been holding it wrong for 8 years too.

paragraphs.body: To describe my saxophone in particular, I imagine the stiffness of the keys. When I played this sax for the first time, it almost felt as if the saxophone was constantly fighting against me to maintain its resting state. I eventually sent it in for servicing and had the tension decreased a little. Yet, it still feels stiffer than any other saxophone I’ve played. Appearance wise my sax has a gold lacquer on the body and bell with a silver neck and keywork. I’ve always liked the look of silver on gold, especially the neck being silver which gives the whole horn a more two-tone look. Since I only own one saxophone, I’ve used it for everything. There are scratches all over the body in various places including a deep long gash that I have no clue where it came from on the underbelly of the bell, scratches all along the right side keywork and lip of the bell which are from placing the instrument down on coarse concrete during marching band, and general blemishes on the neck and bell from nicking my sax on sharp metal music stands. When running your hand over the instrument, apart from the intricate keywork, it is very likely that you would feel some sort of imperfection. The horn itself also has a unique sound for me compared to every other saxophone I’ve played. I find its timbre harsher in the midtones while the higher pitches are more focused and softer.

paragraphs.body: One thing that really pops out on my saxophone is the altissimo which is an extended register of the intended two and a half octave range of the saxophone. It’s a technique often used by classical players to play violin etudes and jazz players to create more versatile solos. These altissimo notes are based in the overtone series which are frequencies that resonate above the underlying original frequency, they’re basically just high ear-piercing notes. I began learning altissimo for a song I was playing with my friends in a sax quartet. The last few measures of the song had an outro solo that went all the way up to a high A, I mainly play alto saxophone, but at the time I was playing soprano for this sax quartet. Because of this, I was not only trying to learn a note almost two whole steps above the highest intended note on a saxophone, I was also starting my learning on the highest pitched standard saxophone of the family. Altissimo is very specific and relies on perfect placement of your lips, tongue, reed, and air stream, so I sat in my room for hours trying out different fingerings and playing the same notes repeatedly. The whole process took about a month to learn it on soprano, but no matter how much I tweaked my mouth I could never get it to sound clean. After I learned it sketchily on the soprano, I began trying it out on my alto and it began to just pop. I don’t know if it’s the fact that mechanisms are sort of wonky and allow for easy note cracking or if the shape of the horn accommodates better for these overtones, but I could just get them out fairly easy and often very clear.

paragraphs.body: Out of everything interesting about my saxophone, the altissimo is the trait I see to be the most unique to the horn itself regardless of player. It’s a very special quality because of that too since most saxophonists played with the same gear but had wildly different sounds. Sidney Bechet for instance, who played the soprano sax, sounded like an opera singer. His rich tone was backed by a ferocious vibrato that was unlike any player before him. While on the other hand, a player like John Coltrane would play the soprano like a jazz vocalist with a raspy tone. Two people playing the same instrument with two very different tones. Even in terms of just general sax sounds there are people that no one can sound like. Tenor sax player James Carter has always made his sax sound like it was screaming in agony and one time he literally did scream into the sax while playing with the Phil Collins Big Band (Collins 19:38). His solo is one of my favorites because of how genre bending it is, at some points it doesn’t even feel like he’s playing a saxophone. To complete contrast of Carter there’s Stan Getz who was known for his laid-back chill tone that probably would never be heard if he was playing on the same stage as James Carter. All of these sax players like Johnny Hodges, Bird, Prez, Gerald Albright, Cannonball, Gerry Mulligan, Paul Desmond, Masato Honda, and so many more all have their own unique sound. I could probably write a whole essay on each one of these artists, but it would be easier to explain why this is possible. Because of how the instrument is built, there are so many variables that go into creating a sound, and uniquely from other instruments, there isn’t really one single “good tone,” the sax can almost fit any style or genre of music. I’ve heard the sax played in solo classical pieces, orchestral pieces, in a wind ensemble, in swing music, bebop, ballads, jazz fusion, rock music, pop music, and even metal music. The sound can be heavily affected by equipment like specialized mouthpieces or reeds that can make the tone darker or brighter but for the most part it’s all in the mouth. Similar to how a player would achieve altissimo, your sound comes from how you shape your lips and place your teeth on the mouthpiece which is referred to as embouchure. Some players achieve a perfectly relaxed and balanced embouchure which results in a beautiful open and warm rich tone that sounds like a cool breeze blowing through a field of tall grass. But if you’re like me and have a bit too tight of an embouchure and choke up the reed at the tip of the mouthpiece, you’ll get a more restricted sound that sounds more like a wooden carriage rolling on a stone road during a blood moon. With each embouchure, there are ups and downs. For example, with the loose shape projecting strong full low notes but making it much harder to pop out those higher ranges of the sax. Then when you look at the tight embouchure which struggles down in the lower register to create strong low notes, you get a bright shining upper register that makes the dark scary stone road not feel all too bad anymore.

paragraphs.body: I think it’s interesting to think about all the differences in tone the sax since there’s only one way to get a sound out of the instrument. I see this as the formula for the sax, and it starts with a player’s embouchure. Everyone who plays naturally knows how to make “their embouchure” but if you ask anyone about it, they’ll give you the same formula. Top teeth on the top side of the mouthpiece, bottom teeth covered by the lower lip are placed on the reed, tongue is right behind that, and the in general the corners of your mouth are tight to create a seal around the mouthpiece. There’s a universal understanding that this is how you make a sound. However, there are so many more variables that go into solving the formula and they all change the answer slightly. It’s like converting temperatures, if you want to go from 25℃ to Fahrenheit you would use the formula, 𝑥 9

paragraphs.body: 5 +32 = 𝑦, where 𝑥 is degrees Celsius and 𝑦 is your answer in degrees Fahrenheit. Then when you alter the value of 𝑥 you obviously end up with a different answer for 𝑦 but, it’s still temperature. If I were to write a formula for sax embouchure it would look like.

𝑆𝑢 𝑃𝑢+ 𝑆𝑙 𝑃𝑙+ 𝐹𝐵(𝑆𝐵) + 𝐹𝐽(𝑆𝐽) + (𝑃𝑇∗𝜃𝑇) = 𝐸 (𝑃𝑀) 𝐹𝐿 The first variable, 𝑃𝑀, stands for placement on mouthpiece. It multiplies all other variables because it is one of the most important factors to consider. Where you place your mouth determines how much airflow is allowed into the saxophone which can drastically change your sound regardless of any other variable. The following section in parentheses includes variables that describe force applied by lip, 𝐹𝐿, which is affected by the shape, 𝑆, and placement, 𝑃, of the upper, 𝑢, and lower lip, 𝑙. The variables force of bite, 𝐹𝐵, affected by the shape of bite, 𝑆𝐵, and the force of jaw, 𝐹𝐽, affected by the shape of jaw, 𝑆𝐽, are also factors that affect how someone’s embouchure sounds. The last two variables describe the position of tongue, 𝑃𝑇, and the angle of tongue, 𝜃𝑇, which both affect the focus of airstream and vibration of the saxophone’s reed. All these variables result in an embouchure, 𝐸, which is unique to that player. Past just embouchure there are many different formulas that can be made for learning new techniques or practicing, many of which I personally created and use.

annotations.comment: Wow. Fascinating!

paragraphs.body: Formula is defined as “A set form of words for use in a ceremony or ritual” (“Formula Definition”). According to etymonline.com, formula originated as a word based “in a ceremony or ritual.” In some cases, I follow less of the mathematic variables approach and use formulas more like rituals. For example, when I first open the case and take out the sax I follow the same formula every time, place the reed in my mouth, put on my neck strap, attach the body to the neck strap, the neck to the body, then the mouthpiece to the neck, and then finally the reed on the mouthpiece. I’ve taken my sax out of the case like this for the longest time that it honestly feels like a ritual. I then warm up the same way every time, I begin with a concert Bb major scale in a normal range than after a few play throughs I begin to move up into the extended range. Afterwards I repeat this with a few other scales along with some major scale arpeggios. In general, I see these formulas as helpful. They’re not restrictions to me but more like tradition. It’s like brushing your teeth before bed every night, you do it every night and because of that it gets you in the mindset to sleep. Similarly, taking out my saxophone like this gets me ready to play.

The only negative connotation I associate with formula is conformity. I like always having a plan but there’s a certain point where it is more confining to stick to a formula in a way that limits my freedom. In band, for example, I had to conform by playing written sheet music that had strict tempo and dynamics. It makes sense because band needs to be in perfect sync to sound good however, it’s just not as fun in my opinion. What the saxophone is known for is jazz and improv and coming up with things on the spot. It runs outside of the strict formula of sheet music but still follows the looser formula of a chord structure. You’re playing the same chords that the song has but you come up with new melodies and harmonies. Charlie Parker’s song “Ko Ko” still has a formula behind it; it uses the same structure as Ray Noble’s “Cherokee”, but Parker didn’t conform; he instead came up with his own melody. According to Parker himself, he was “bored with the stereotyped changes being used then” (Komara 1). After playing through the tune he stated that “there’s bound to be something else,” and that “[he] could hear it sometimes, but [he] couldn’t play it” (Komara 2). The melody that he envisioned is considered to be one of the first tunes for the genre of Bop, which was the style of music that made Parker famous. Parkers’ decision to expand and create based on the formula of Cherokee rather than confine is what gave birth to one of the most influential genres in the history of jazz and solidified his name as one of the most influential sax players of all.

Formulas help me beyond the saxophone in the rest of my life. I typically will use a formula to prepare myself for conversations to either finish them quickly or extend them. The formula sheet on a math test is my best friend, formulas in general create order and stability, as well as providing guidelines when I have no idea what I’m doing. I relate a lot of what I do with the saxophone to my real life still though because I really should practice saxophone more and I should probably be talking to people more too. The more I write this paper the more I think about how much I’ve planned my life out. I know most of what I want to do, and I have a clear path of how I’m going to get it. It’s made me think about how much of my life would be different if I just rolled with the punches without any formulas. In a research paper written by Esra Altıntaş and Şükrü Ilgün on the opinions of mathematics teachers in Turkey on Formulas in math, 62.89 percent of teachers answered “No” to the question “Is there mathematics without formula and rule?” (Altıntaş and Ilgün). It definitely isn’t realistic to say I wouldn’t exist if I didn’t have formulas, but I do think a significant amount of my life would be different.
